Sample Answers 1 To: The board of Vatten From: Performance management expert Date: 1 September 20X5 Subject:Performance issues at Vatten This report evaluates the suitability of a performance dashboard suggested by the consultant.It then benchmarks the performance of Vatten's e-commerce function against Snizort and advises on the problems of benchmarking against Snizort.Finall,the report provides advice on the use of cloud-based computing as a strategy to improve performance. (i) Evaluation of the suitability of the proposed performance dashboard The board members,who started the business,appear to share most managerial responsibilities,other than those undertaken by the finance manager,as the other employees work in the warehouse or as drivers.They need a range of operational and strategic data to help them run the business and manage performance.Vatten's broad aims for the business are to: - Grow the brand to be the best in the Beeland eco-fashion industry. - Reduce Vatten's impact on the environment. In addition,there are several key issues which Vatten is facing: - E-commerce system downtime due to due to infection by computer viruses. - Basic e-commerce systems compared to those of competitors'systems. - Very weak cash flow. Grow the brand to be the best in the Beeland eco-fashion industry The finance and operations part of the dashboard presents revenue and transaction volume as key performance indicators. These could be interpreted as measuring the growth of the brand,though the definition of this aim is unclear. The average online customer review score,shown in the customer area,relates to the aim to grow the brand in the sense of how customers perceive the brand.It covers a range of factors which customers will value and will help Vatten measure performance in this area.Some measures such as trust in the brand are poorly defined and subjective.This data will not inform Vatten how to improve performance in these areas. The competitors'part of the dashboard appears to be able to sort retailers according to various aspects of their performance, such as the average online review score.This is one measure of being the best in the industry.Vatten could clearly see its position relative to competitors and the industry average.Market share is one measure of growing the brand and an indirect measure of being the best. The average item price does not directly relate to the aims or the key issues but is operational data which can be used to ensure Vatten's product prices remain competitive in the fast-moving eco-fashion industry. Reduce Vatten's impact on the environment The reduction of Vatten's impact on the environment would be of interest to customers,but no measures for this are shown in the customer area on the dashboard. The company has a good reputation as a socially responsible brand and has the reduction of its impact on the environment as one of its broad aims.Because of this,it is important to include measures relating to Vatten's impact on the environment in the dashboard. E-commerce system downtime due to infection by computer viruses The graphics showing the current daily rates of revenue generation and transaction volumes are of lileuse as they are very short-term indicators and there is no link,for example,to any target.The terms low and high are subjective and poorly defined. A low transaction volume may indicate system downtime due to virus infections,but otherwise there is no measure or indicator relating to this issue. Key ratios,such as the percentage of e-commerce system downtime,would help identify the extent of this problem. Basic e-commerce systems compared to those of competitors'systems There is no direct data on the dashboard relating to this key issue which Vatten is facing.This would require the inclusion of external data about competitors,for example,the amount spent on e-commerce systems relative to their revenue.Comparisons may be meaningless because of the much larger size of some competitors. Also,it is difficult to measure whether a system is basic as this is a subjective evaluation. Weak cash flow There are currently no metrics relating to cash flow on the dashboard.Vatten has very weak cash flow,though it is unclear why. This may be because the board is focusing on growing the brand,at the expense of managing cash flow.Vatten is a young company and the board may not be expecting it to generate positive net cash flow yet.The dashboard does,however,need to include measures relating to cash flow if Vatten is to survive and be able to invest in new IT systems. ii)Benchmarking exercise Security It is not possible to benchmark security against Snizort,because it has not given any data relating to the number of security breach events,probably because it is commercially and legally sensitive.Even if it had,the benchmark is not useful because 'an event'is too broadly defined.The detection of a virus during a routine scan is a trivial event which may cause no harm or disruption.Theft of customers'data by hackers is a much more serious security breach which could have major legal and reputational implications. There may also be regulatory sanctions or fines,for example,for breaching data security regulations.Vatten has already received two fines in the year from the Beeland authorities fllowing theft of customers'personal data by hackers,whereas Snizort has not received any.Snizort is apparently performing better than Vatten in this respect,though the size of the fines and their materiality to Vatten is unknown. Reliability The number of downtime days is a measure of reliability.Snizort appears to outperform Vatten(53 days v91 days).It seems, however,that 52 out of Snizort's 53 days may be due to the weekly shutdown of its systems for planned maintenance,at a time which would cause minimal disruption.This would make Snizor's performance seem very good.The usefulness of this benchmark is limited because the duration of the downtime and its impact on customers are unknown.As with security,there are cost and reputational impacts of downtime,which cannot be compared because there is no data given on this. Because Snizort largely outsources its e-commerce system,security and reliability may largely be the responsibility of thee external supplier.Snizort may still,however,retain control over some aspects of this as it employs a part-time e-commerce manager.Snizort may also have chosen what levels of system security and redundancy it was prepared to pay the external supplier for. Cost effectiveness Vatten out-performs Snizort in terms of cost-effectiveness.Its total e-commerce cost per transaction is $0·48,much lower than Snizort at $0·74(W1).Snizort largely outsources its e-commerce system,as 78%(35+78)/145)of its e-commerce costs are paid to external suppliers.Vatten may not view this benchmark as useful because it is appearing to be performing relatively well,but it does suggest a different way of providing this function,which Vatten should investigate. Overall,Snizort's cost per transaction is 54%higher than Vatten's($0·74v$0·48)which is a large difference in percentage terms,though a small absolute difference.However,though Snizort's cost per transaction is higher,each transaction will also be of much higher value than for Vatten.This means that the cost to Snizort for each online transaction will be much lower relative to the revenue generated.In this respect,Snizort's e-commerce system could be said to be the more cost effective. 54%(78/145)of Snizort's e-commerce costs are variable costs,whereas all Vatten's costs are fixed.This would mean that Snizort would achieve a small cost saving if demand were to fall,whereas Vatten's costs would remain unchanged.Conversely, Vatten's costs would remain unchanged if demand were to increase in the future. Overall,though Snizor's cost per transaction is higher,it seems to benefit from better security,as it has no fines for breaches of data security regulations.It also appears to have less downtime,given the limitations of the measure given. W1-Cost effectiveness E-commerce function costs Vatten ($) Snizort ($) Depreciation of e-commerce hardware and software 50,000 20,000 Finance costs of e-commerce equipment 10,000 Finance manager's salary relating to e-commerce(W2) 18,000 一 Fixed annual payment to external suppliers 一 35,000 Variable cost payable to external suppliers(195,000x$0·40) Part-time e-commerce system manager's salary 一 78,000 12,000 Total e-commerce costs 78,000 145,000 Number of online transactions 163,000 195,000 Measure: Total IT cost per online transaction $0·48 $0·74 W2-Finance manager's salary relating to e-commerce 50%of the time is spent on IT systems and 60%of this time relates to e-commerce: $60,000x50%x60%=$18,000 Note: The requirement could also have been interpreted as $60,000×60%=$36,000.This would result in an IT cost per online transaction of $0·59.This would also have been marked correct,including follow on marks. Problems of benchmarking with Snizort A problem of benchmarking is that it relies on obtaining accurate and comparable data.It is unclear how accurate the data from Snizort is,or whether it is measured on a basis consistent with that of Vatten. Snizort derives 65%of its revenue from online sales and so it has a significant proportion of offline sales.The data may not be comparable,for example,because sales in retail shops may not require as complex or expensive IT systems as sales made online.This would make the results of the benchmarking exercise misleading. Though Snizort has a similar number of online transactions,as a furniture retailer,individual transactions will be of much higher value.Snizort may be so much larger than Vatten that it is not a suitable benchmark.A company more similar in size and nature should be chosen as a benchmark. As an objective of benchmarking is to improve performance to match the benchmark,it is important to choose a benchmark which represents good or even best in class performance.It is unclear whether this applies to Snizort. As the benchmarking scheme is a government scheme,the participants are chosen by the Beeland government.Vatten, therefore,has no choice about which company to benchmark against.It is unclear why Snizort was chosen as a benchmark. Vatten is attempting functional benchmarking with an external organisation,Snizort.A problem with external benchmarking is the reluctance of organisations to share confidential data which may be useful to competitors.The terms of the government scheme should prevent this,but Snizort has sill chosen not to share data relating to the number of security breaches,as this may be legally or commercially sensitive.Even if both participants in the benchmarking exercise are willing to share confidential data,such as the number of times their IT systems have been hacked,they may not even know how many times this had happened.This would make the data unreliable and misleading. Snizort may benefit from economies of scale which are unavailable to Vatten.Trying to match Snizort's performance may de-motivate managers,particularly the finance manager,who has responsibility for maintaining the e-commerce system.If hee is trying to achieve a level of performance which is unrealistic,he may give up trying to improve performance altogether as a result. Focusing on improvement against the factors benchmarked could distract attention away from other areas.Though IT is clearly an important issue to Vatten,due to competitors'access to superior technology,it faces other issues which may be of more immediate importance,such as very weak cash flow.The benchmarking exercise will take a lot of management time and Vatten has few managers,only the board and the finance manager. (i ii)Improvement of performance by using a cloud-based computing provider(CCP) Vatten needs to upgrade its e-commerce system because its competitors are investing their large financial resources in improving their own systems in order to use advanced techniques,such as data mining and algorithms.This will give them a large competitive advantage over Vatten. Cash flow Vatten is a relatively new and small company with weak cash flow,which may restrict its access to borrowings.It probably cannot afford capital expenditure on new e-commerce systems.By using a CCP,the capital expenditure which would otherwise be required to purchase equipment and manage it in-house will be replaced by an ongoing operating expense.In addition, often the cost of system upgrades and new hardware and software are included in the contracts.Vatten may also see energy consumption costs reduced.Buying an e-commerce system requires large upfront investment,so using a CCP could improve cash flow and enable Vatten to have access to the e-commerce systems required. Vatten would only pay for the e-commerce services provided by the CCP according to the amount of usage,which will match the costs to the level of activity.This avoids paying for expensive facilities which are not required in the early stages of expansion. For example,itcan be seen from the benchmarking exercise with Snizort that the external supplier,to which they outsource their e-commerce system,charges a fixed annual amount and a relatively low variable charge of $0·40 for each online transaction.As one of Vatten's broad aims is to grow the brand,this presumably means that the company plans to increase the number of online transactions.By using a CCP,Vatten may be able to increase the volume of online transactions for a relatively small variable cost,rather than incurring larger stepped costs for additional staff and equipment as volumes increase. The finance manager balances 50%of his time maintaining the company's IT systems,of which 60%relates to the e-commerce system.He therefore spends 12 hours a week(40×50%×60%)maintaining the e-commerce system.He wants to work 12 hours less each week due to family commitments.If a CCP was used,the company could potentially save $18,000 (60,000×50%x60%)of the finance manager's salary. Scalability Vatten's existing e-commerce system is not scalable as it is close to the limit of the number of online transactions it can process each day The CCP would benefit from economies of scale compared to Vatten having its own e-commerce system which potentially means that these economies could be passed on to Vatten,hence improving financial performance.As Vatten expands,thee capacity of the IT systems can be easily scaled up,without the need to purchase additional IT equipment or employ more staff.A CCP allows companies to scale up or scale down their system needs,which provides flexibility.This is beneficial to the performance of Vatten as the company aims to grow the brand,which will mean an increase in the volume of online transactions.The CCP will undertake purchasing and installing upgrades as required,which willallow Vatten to focus on running its business. Access to expertise and resources Unlike large online retailers,who have the expertise and large financial resources to continuously invest in improving their e-commerce systems,Vatten does not have the capability to develop and maintain a sophisticated e-commerce system. Using a CCP would give them access to specialist expertise in order to be able to do this and so reduce Vatten's competitive disadvantage in this area. Outsourcing support of the e-commerce system to a CCP would also free up the finance manager's time to address other key issues in the business or focus on finance work. As an online retailer,IT security is a key risk to Vatten.The company has already suffered downtime due to infection by computer viruses.A benefit of using a CCP is that it would reduce the risk of IT security breaches,which could have serious consequences for the company's financial performance or even its survival,given the current very weak cash flow. Security breaches may tarnish Vatten's reputation as a socially responsible brand.It could lose customers and incur fines or legal consequences.A CCP would have the systems,expertise and resources to prevent and investigate security breaches, which would not be available to Vatten if it purchased and managed a new system or retained the existing one. In addition,if there were any business continuity issues like a power failure,then having data stored in the cloud ensures it is backed up and protected.If Vatten is then able to access its data again quickly,this reduces downtime and it can get back to business as usual. Some of Vatten's customers are located in different time zones,which may mean the e-commerce system requires support for 24 hours every day.An external CCP may be able to provide this,whereas this would be harder for a system supported just by Vatten staff working in Beeland. The use of a CCP may also have overall environmental benefits,for example,due to the CCP having up-to-date energy efficient IT systems or by avoiding the need for duplication of physical resources,such as IT hardware. 2 (a) Big data is generally perceived to be:extremely large collections of data (data sets)which may be analysed to reveal patterns, trends,and associations,especially relating to human behaviour and interactions.It refers to data which is so large,fast or complex that it is difficult or impossible to process using traditional methods. Variety Variety describes data which does not fit into a standard format such as an excel spreadsheet.The data which Curra will obtain and intends to maintain on an on-going basis will come in many formats as it includes internet searches and social media communications.Such communication may comprise various methods,such as individuals'comments,or them denoting their interest or approval for something by use of an emoji or like'button or similar,and/or by them posting photographs and using media such as GIFs and memes.Curra would need to find a way of dealing with the different variety of these data sources. Even the written comments could vary in the format they are produced,from individual words or brief interjections,such as question marks,to longer more developed paragraphs.There may be difiulties for Curra in addressing and structuring these diferent types of written communication.Additionally,the software which it intends to purchase seems to be useful for thee analysis of written data only,not for analysing data which has not been communicated in writing.This may leave Cura with a significant problem as to how it might analyse individuals'posting of photographs and,specifically,whether such postings can be used to denote those individuals'desire for additional goods and services which may require a loan.This analysis may have to be undertaken manually,which would seem to be a lengthy task. Velocity Velocity describes a situation where data is generated at a fast speed and often in real time.This will present a significant challenge for Curra as the usage of the internet and of social media in general are phenomena which are rising on a global level.For Curra's customer targeting to be up-to-date and tailored to individual customer requirements on an on-going basis, Curra will need to be able to process the data in real time and then target customers through social media.To do this, Curra will require much more than the data analysis software which it is likely to purchase as this software only identifies emerging themes through one form of data and does not appear to contain further ability to use the data and specifically target individuals.It would appear that Curra still has to make that decision on a manual basis and,given the speed at which the data is being produced,a manual system could not keep pace with this.Curra would need to purchase further software which uses the analysis of the data to simultaneously target Curra's prospective customers with its adverts.It may also need to purchase hardware,such as servers and processors,which can ensure data is processed at the required rate. Volume Volume was the initial primary metric of describing big data and refers to the previously incomprehensible amounts of data which are produced.The volume of data which Curra will rquire to process and analyse is already vast and is likely to increase further as the internet searches the citizens of Zeeland undertake could be expected to increase as competition inall markets increases.There may also be an increase in the use of social media as citizens communicate more with each other about their choices as consumers.There is likely to be a great deal of data which is irelevant to Curra and it may be time-consuming for the software package to filter this to include only data which is relevant and useful for Curra.Additionally,the exact nature of the software which Curra will purchase is unclear but it is likely that,even ifit is fit for purpose when purchased,it will become obsolete and will be less able to process changing volumes and types of data.This is likely to mean that Curra will either have to pay to upgrade the software on a regular basis or purchase new software. The volume of data is also likely to create significant storage problems for Curra and it will have to create and maintain extensive data warehouses for this purpose.Curra does not have experience of maintaining this volume of data and this is likely to involve significant software and human resources expense.Given the developments in government policy,it is possible that Curra may need to maintain that data for compliance purposes also. Veracity Veracity refers to the quality of the data,specifically whether it is accurate and reliable.This could be a significant problem for Curra and there are likely to be two major challenges here.First,Curra is not intending (or is simply not able)to target customers who have already expressed a desire to purchase a financial services product but instead is making assumptions about the customers'needs for these products,from communications those customers have undertaken.For example,if thee software recognises expressions of desire for more consumer goods,then it may register communication around something such as a relatively inexpensive item such as a shirt as a reason to target these consumers with messages about Curra's products.However,the purchase of a shirt would be an unlikely reason to seek a loan and Curra may find that it has significant challenges in ensuring that the data it has is useful for the end purpose. The second challenge which Curra has is that,with markets opening up to increased competition,the search engine which Curra is obtaining its information from may not be the one which the majority of the citizens of Zeeland are using on an on-going basis.It is very possible,given the change from a dictatorship to a democracy,that the citizens of Zeeland will change how they search the internet as the older search engine may be seen as symbolic of the past,rather than the future.If that is the case,then Curra may be receiving data which,increasingly,does not cover the bulk of the people whom it would wish to target. There is also a concern with regard to the age of the data.Some of it could be up to six years old,which would mean that it is unlikely to be helpful now for Curra in targeting its advertising to a body of consumers who might need a loan to satisfy a current desire to purchase something such as a holiday,for example.Curra might be better advised to try to purchase only more recent data (for example,from the last two years),though the additional data may allow Curra to build up a more complete picture of the consumers they are hoping to advertise to. (b)Ethical issues around the use of the data There are several ethical issues involved in Curra's intended purchase and use of the data.First,the data which Curra intends to purchase has come from individuals'personal internet searches and personal communications.It is most likely that those individuals did not know their internet searches were being monitored by a consulting company and,therefore,their personal interests and searches should belong to the individuals alone and should not be disclosed without their expressed permission. The use of individuals'data and communications from mobile phones is perhaps worse,from an ethical perspective,as people would not expect that such personal communication was being monitored by any external body. Similarly,the individuals'contacts on social media have not been asked if their details can be used and these individuals have thereby had personal information sold without their permission.The scenario does not elaborate on the distinct nature of thee social media platforms which have been used,nor of the nature and purpose of the communication,but it is likely that some or much of this communication,like the communication via mobile phone,could have been of a personal nature and particularly sensitive.Individuals may have been communicating about potential changes in family circumstances,for example,and Curra's ability to know about this and to offer a loan,or extended credit cards terms,based on this knowledge is unethical. These concerns should give the CEO cause for concern that the data itself has been obtained by unethical means. Specific issues around the ethical issues raised by the charities It is clear that the charities in Zeeland believe that the use of data to target vulnerable individuals is unethical as the adverts which these individuals see are likely to influence them to take on debt which they cannot afford.The charities have highlighted increased numbers of citizens using their services as evidence of this and it is clear the charities feel this increase is creating a very significant social problem which they are increasingly unequipped to deal with. As a listed company,Curra has a duty to return value to its shareholders,who are the main investors in the company.However, it has to do so responsibly and with integrity.Such behaviour should help to ensure the sustainability of its business model (for example,by developing a good reputation among customers)and enhance its ability to return value to its shareholders over time. Currently,it appears as though Curra would use the data to attempt to specifically target particular groups of individuals,many of whom appear to be vulnerable.By using the data in this way,it appears as though Curra is trying to seek out vulnerable groups and exploit their position in order to boost its own profitability.There is also already quite clear evidence that lending of this nature is leading to significant social cost,which Curra could be deemed to be contributing towards. It is important to note that it is Curra's intended use of the data which is unethical and not the product(i.e.the loan)itself.Thee product may be something which the government of Zeeland decides to regulate in the future but there are no doubt many cases where the product itself offers a valuable and necessary source of income to those who may be in short-term financial dificulty.However,using the data to deliberately seek out those whom Curra is fully aware are in a vulnerable position is unethical and Curra shoud not use the data for this purpose. 3 (a) There is no indication in Borralan's corporate steering wheel of how different levels of the organisation relate to each other or which department might have ownership of the measures.It does not show how any measures support or interlink with each other.f it was a genuine corporate steering wheel,then these are most likely the measures being reported to the board.Some, such as on-time delivery,seem too operational in this regard. Borralan's current objective is to maximise shareholder value,based on a vision of supplying tyres of excellent quality and being known throughout Teeland for its value.It is questionable whether either the objective or the vision is being adequately assessed. Market Sales volume may be an appropriate measure of market share in a growing market.However,the market in Teeland is growing only slightly and there must be a genuine concern for a company which is not at the forefront of current developments such as Borralan that increasing sales volume is being achieved by reducing the selling price.This would appear to be supported by increased revenue in Borralan during the last year at a reduced net profit margin.The focus on sales volume as a performance measure is likely to have encouraged sub-optimal behaviour at Borralan in terms of maximising returns in shareholder value, as it appears to be keen to cut the selling price in a market which is beginning to change quite fundamentally in terms of thee goods consumers are looking for. Financial The relation between net profit margin and maximising shareholder value is only slight although net profit margin can give an adequate representation of cost control in Borralan.However,in a competitive marketplace where activities such as product development are important,a focus on net profit margin is likely to lead to a reduction in such expenditure.There is little or no relation between gross profit margin and shareholder value,as this measure only addresses the direct costs of manufacture. Customer satisfaction Market satisfaction is driven by customer satisfaction and Borralan's measure of this,based on complaints,is inadequate. Measuring complaints will not tellBorralan how satisfied customers are with its products or whether they represent good value. It is a very reactive measure which will only cover a relatively small group of people who are unhappy enough to make a complaint.It is significant that other companies in the industry have been proactive in seeking customer views on what features they would like to have in tyres and have,as a result,been innovative in their subsequent design.Borralan's ignorance of the market and customers is highlighted by the production manager who had no knowledge of the new features which appear to be redefining the market for tyres. Flexibility Flexibility in a manufacturing company is usually measured through aspects such as time to market new designs or response time.There is no measure for that in Borralan's corporate steering wheel though such a measure does seem particularly appropriate given the current developments in the marketplace. Productivity Productivity is being measured by the number of tyres produced/maximum capacity.It is not clear how the maximum capacity has been derived but it is likely that it is this metric which is leading to a focus on trying to make the process of production shorter.This measure also leads to a focus on enhancing volume,perhaps at the expense of quality,when it is clear that thee main issue in the company is that it is no longer producing tyres which consumers want.This measure is therefore likely to lead to a reduction in shareholder value and is driven by an internal focus on capacity utilisation which does not relate to the company's overall objective. Quality There is currently no measure of quality though it is clear that Borralan would benefit from a focus in this area.It appears as though quality is being checked only after production has finished when it would be preferable for this to be undertaken atall stages of the production process. Delivery Delivery is currently being measured through on-time delivery. Cycle time Cycle time is not being measured through the current corporate steering wheel.Given that flexibility is becoming increasingly important,it is now imperative that Borralan develops measures which reflect the significance of this area.Borralan perhaps needs to undertake some kind of process improvement or redevelopment as previous attempts to try to speed up the process of manufacture have resulted in significant quality problems. Waste Waste is not being measured currently by Borralan but appears to be a significant problem for it.The scenario indicates that Borralan's attempts to shorten the production process have had an effect on the quality of the final product which is likely to result in an increased number of rejected products.The measurement of waste would perhaps lead to process improvement also as it would force Borralan to ask fundamental questions about its processes and how they might be enhanced to reduce waste. (b) The bonus schemes currently in place in Borralan have led to sub-optimal performance as different parts of the company have pursued set targets at the expense of other company objectives.Neither of them is aligned to the company's objective of maximising shareholder value. Production department The production department is encouraged to produce as many tyres as possible.Perhaps this is the reason for previous attempts to quicken the production process with the resulting quality problems.There is no indication that these tyres are thee right products,something which may threaten Borralan's existence if its bonus system is encouraging the build-up of inventory which is no longer required.The nature of fixed cost absorption would also encourage the company to produce as many tyres as possible to keep the unit price as low as possible.In short,the bonus and reward system here,though very clear,is damaging to Borralan's prospects at least in the short term.Such a scheme can work well in a growing market when quantity produced translates easily into revenue and profit,but the newer tyres take longer to produce,are clearly more bespoke and perhaps rewards based on something such as number of defects would be better. Managerial staff Awarding a bonus to managerial staff based on revenue,as with the other bonus system,may work well in a growing market where it could easily be assumed that revenue translates into profit.However,in the past year it does appear that revenue has been enhanced at the expense of profitability,which is contrary to the company's objective.It is quite naive for the board of Borralan to assume that there is a direct relationship between enhancing revenue and maximising shareholder value,and this is a dangerous basis on which to award a bonus in Borralan's marketplace.It will focus management attention on encouraging sales of current inventory to enhance revenue,possibly at a loss as the market for Borralan's products declines. Revenue is often used as a proxy measure for market share as it is assumed that an increase in revenue will most likely result in an increase in market share.This is also potentially damaging for Borralan,given that the nature of its market is changing. The bonus system may be encouraging managerial staff to ignore the innovations taking place externally in the industry and instead to focus on increasing revenue in a market which is shrinking,and which is less profitable than it has been in thee past.Instead,it would be preferable to reward managerial staff by forcing them to focus on the changing environment and by encouraging them to be innovative.A measure such as number of new products brought to market would be more helpful in this regard. Conclusion Taken together,the two bonus systems are now dangerously inappropriate for the changing market conditions Borralan finds itself in.The production department is being motivated to produce tyres which will most likely become outdated very quickly and management is being encouraged to focus only on revenue which will inevitably decline quite quickly.Neither system has an external focus.If things stay the same,it looks as though Borralan's existence may be threatened. Benchmarking exercise clearly addresses the three areas and makes effective use of the data supplied Scepticism: - Recognition that Vatten will need a range of operational and strategic data to run the business and manage performance - Problems of benchmarking with Snizort are clearly identified for Vatten and considers a range of issues Commercial acumen: - Advice on the use of a CCP is focused on Vatten and addresses valid commercial points Maximum 10 marks Total 50 marks 2 (a) Each ‘V'-up to 4 marks per V,1 mark per relevant point Maximum 10 marks (b) Ethical concerns: Ethical issues involved in the purchase of the data generally-1 mark per relevant point up to a maximum of 7 Ethical issues raised by the charities-1 mark per relevant point,up to a maximum of 7 Maximum 10 marks Professional skills marks Analysis and evaluation: Evaluation of the 4Vs appropriately applied to the problems Curra may experience,clearly supported with relevant examples relating to Cura Scepticism: Assessment of the ethical issues addresses both the problems of purchase and use of personal data and shows consideration of the implications for Curra Commercial acumen: - Recognition that much of the data captured will be irrelevant for Curra - Identification that Curra has a responsibility to its shareholders Maximum 5 marks Total 25 marks 3 (a) Assessment of the measures-1 mark per point,up to 13 marks Maximum 13 marks (b) Production staff bonus-1 mark per point,up to 4 marks Managerial staff bonus-1 mark per point,up to 4 marks Maximum 7 marks Professional skills marks Analysis and evaluation: Comprehensive and structured assessment of the measures in relation to the performance pyramid is provided Scepticism: 一 Recognition is made of the areas in the performance pyramid which the existing measures do not cover Commercial acumen: - Recognition of the lack of hierarchy/linkage in the corporate steering wheel - Current reward systems are adequately assessed in terms of their alignment to Borralan's objective Maximum 5marks Total 25 marks Answers Strategic Professional-Options,APM Advanced Performance Management(APM) September/December 2024 Sample Answers 1 To: The board of Jayland Medicines Regulator(JMR) From: A Accountant Date: 1 September 20X5 Subject:Performance measurement and management issues at JMR This report assesses the work already performed by the consultant on the BSC.The consultant's interpretation of the BSC customer, internal business process and innovation and learning perspectives are assessed,followed by a detailed evaluation of their suggested KPls for these perspectives.It goes on to explain value for money (VFM)and evaluate whether the suggested financial KPls measure this.Finally,it addresses the concerns of the board about the concept of a lean information system and its implementation. (i)Evaluate the consultant's BSC The consultant has identified the headings for the BSC;however,their interpretation is open to criticism. First,the customer perspective should not be limited to only the government.The objectives of JMR also require consideration of the people of Jayland and the pharmaceutical companies which develop the medicines.It is this variety of important stakeholders(with possibly conflicting objectives)which makes performance measurement difficult for a public body such as JMR. The KPls under this perspective do not,however,seem to relate directly to the government and instead,they relate to public health.The life expectancy is of primary interest to the people of Jayland although it will be driven by many more factors than just the medicines available (such as diet).Therefore,this indicator does not accurately reflect only the work of JMR.Also, it would be more useful,since the aim is to 'improve',to look at the change in health indicators so the percentage change of 0·1%is a better guide. The second indicator again has limitations.It does not cover the medicines which were incorrectly rejected by JMR and so,the lost benefit of these.A measure which compares with other countries'regulators would be more effective.Also,this indicator seems to be affected by single events (such as the bad batch in 20X4).It may be more appropriate to look at the number of batches which were authorised but proved ultimately dangerous since this measures the actual errors byJMR. Second,the consultant correctly identifies that the internal business process perspective should focus on the processes which help to achieve the financial and customer objectives but then they do not translate theJMR objectives into process measures. For example,there is no measure reflecting the education of the public in the risks of using the medicines. The indicators for this perspective relate to the processes of authorisation of medicines and staff recruitment.The process of authorisation is limited to only measuring those medicines which were accepted,while it is just as important for JMR to reject unacceptable medicines and so the measure should reflect this by looking at the‘average time to conclude on the acceptability of a medicine'.On the second KPI,while there is an obvious need in this sector for qualified staff it is surprising that this is considered a key measure without it being noted as a problem or objective for JMR. Third,the innovation and learning perspective should really reflect the ability to adapt to the changing environment and yet, the KPls have no reference to external benchmarks or how the technological environment is changing outside of JMR. However,the first KPI does reflect the number of new medicine applications,which relates directly to the importance for JMR to encourage innovation in pharmaceutical companies.The comparison to a five-year average is useful as improvements in performance will take time to have effect but again an external comparator would be very valuable.The need for trained staf measures the capacity of the organisation to address a changing environment and so is also appropriate but needs to be more clearly linked to objectives to be considered a 'key'indicator. Generally,it is notable that there is no measurement of the objective relating to the public understanding of medicines. (ii)Delivering value for money The VFM view appears initilly to be the appropriate financial perspective for an organisation such as JMR which is not driven by profit but by the government's other objectives.It is normally measured by the 3Es of economy,efficiency and effectiveness as illustrated by the consultant's report.However,it should be noted that there are problems in measuring effectiveness purely financially at JMR(see below). Economy Economy measures the cost in money or resources of the inputs into the processes of the organisation. The measure of economy chosen looks at the total cost of running the organisation in absolute terms.This does not,however, reflect the volume of work which it undertakes and so,it is a crude measure of inputs to the organisation.A better measure would involve the key costs,which,at a knowledge-based organisation such as JMR,is likely to relate to staf.So average staff cost could be appropriate as this could then be compared to other nations'medicine regulators. Efficiency This is measured by the efficiency of the processes of the organisation in turning the inputs into its outputs. The measure suggested here appears to be a good one as it relates to the input costs being turned into the output of thee organisation(authorised medicines).However,the output of JMR is not necessarily successful authorisations,so it should also include failed medicines(where failure protects the public from harm).So,an improved measure might be the average time it takes to test a medicine during the year. Effectiveness This is measured by the degree to which the outputs of the organisation achieve its overall objectives. This heading does not sit appropriately within the financial perspective for JMR.JMR's objectives are predominantly non-financial and so it would be more sensible to consider VFM effectiveness from within the customer and innovation perspectives,in so far as it addresses the first three objectives.The specific measure here is therefore too narrow to achieve an understanding of VFM. However,the measure is likely to be a useful measure for the government of Jayland as it wants to see JMR work without public subsidy and so might be considered a useful part of the financial perspective for the BSC.One detailed improvement might be to simplify the measure to just be the year-on-year change in the percentage of income derived from external fees,since it is the change rather than the absolute fraction which would appear important if the government wants JMR to move to financial independence. (iii)New information system The fundamental idea of a lean system is that there is a correct place for everything in an organisation and everything should be in that place.The 5Ss are used to create an organisation which is ordered,where the staff have pride in that order and this system assists their production of high-quality work. Taking each of the 5Ss in turn and illustrating how each idea can be realised within the new information system: Sorting (or structurising)removes any item or process which does not add value in the final output.This would involve removing or archiving files which are not immediately needed.It would ensure that the flow of work through the system did not involve unnecessary or repeated activities.The creation of a unified database,where data is entered only once,will help to achieve this.At JMR,there may be performance data which is no longer needed as a result of the change in objectives and move to the BSC. Simplifying(or systemising)suggests that all items needed for work should be easily and quickly accessible.In the information system,this could involve simplifying applications and files so that those most used are most readily accessible.It would also involve deciding on how to access information whether via an icon or button or through a(slower)search. Scanning(or sanitising)involves identifying the causes of clutter in the workplace and removing these.For information systems, this could involve removing sections of reports which are no longer used or disabling functions which generate unnecessary copies of data or files.It could also involve ensuring that reports and information are only accessible to those levels of thee organisation which need to use them,for example,operational level staff need not have access to board reports. Standardising involves creating a process where it is easier to maintain consistent standards.For example,standardisation could involve having a set timetable for the production of reports for decision-making. Sustaining relates to the motivation of the staff to keep the environment lean.This would involve performance audits by supervisors and vigilance by the staf.This is often the most dificult of the 5Ss to achieve as it requires continualeffort and thee involvement of everyone in the organisation. The difficulties in implementing such an approach at JMR will involve avoiding the exercise being seen as a one-off cleaning up of processes.The approach is intended to be a change of organisational behaviour and so the sustain phase is critical to achieve this.The fact that work will flow through an information system gives great power to the designers of the system to control future work practices and so offers an opportunity to make the change sustainable. 2 (a)Divisional manager's bonus Overall problems with the basis used for performance assessment The manager should only be held accountable for aspects of divisional performance which are within his control.Not doing this would be unfair to him and may lead to him becoming demotivated to improve divisional performance.This is because he may be prevented from receiving his bonus due to non-controllable factors.The performance information relating to the bonus of the manager of T division does not distinguish between aspects of divisional performance which are within the manager's control and aspects which are not. Basing the manager's bonus on the divisional net profit provided in the appendix makes the manager accountable for some factors outside his control.This is because the performance information given does not eliminate,or adjust for,these factors when deciding whether he should receive his bonus.Though the information includes a brief commentary on some of the key variances against the budget,it does not explicitly show which of these are controllable by the manager. It may also be difficult to determine exactly which factors are fully within the manager's control.This could be because thee manager's decisions may be influenced by others'decisions,such as those of the head office,or because of external events or market factors which affect costs and revenue but are not as a result of the manager's decisions alone. The performance shows the T$25,000 adverse variance overall,which means the manager would not receive his bonus, based on profit attributable to the division.In order to advise on whether the divisional manager should receive a bonus, the performance information will need to be adjusted to eliminate the factors outside the manager's control.This adjusted divisional net profit should then be compared to a similarly adjusted budgeted net profit to see whether the target has been achieved or not. Advice on whether the divisional manager should receive his bonus The costs and variances on the performance information will be evaluated below to determine whether they are contrllable by the divisional manager,and therefore whether they should be included when assessing if the manager should receive his bonus: Head office costs Though head office costs are attributable to the division and should therefore be considered when assessing divisional performance,they need to be excluded when assessing the performance of the divisional manager.The manager does not control either the level of these costs,or the basis on which they are allocated,and so T$220,000 should be added back to divisional net profit for the assessment of his performance. However,the budgeted net profit for T division included T$190,000 of head office costs allocated to T division,so this amount also needs to be added back to the budgeted net profit to ensure that the comparison between the adjusted net profit and thee budgeted net profit is done on a consistent basis.The budgeted net profit to which the actual net profit should be compared is T$690,000 (500+190). Gas costs The significant increase in the market price of gas on 1 January 20X5 led to an adverse variance of T$55,000 in gas costs for the year.The divisional manager could not control this adverse variance as he has to accept the market price of gas and thee variable rate tariff was negotiated by head office and not by him. The manager would,however,have ultimate control of maintenance of the gas heaters,which accounted for an adverse variance of T$42,000 and it is correct to take account of this extra cost when assessing the manager's performance.However, the T$55,000 part of the overall adverse variance on gas relating to the gas price increase should be added back to net profit. Cost of sales-Invertors The T$46,000 favourable variance due to exchange rate movements is outside the manager's control,because treasury is a head office function.This variance should be deducted from net profit when assessing the manager's performance. The adverse variance of T$50,000 due to invertors being purchased at a T$10·00 premium for an alternative supplier appears to be under the control of the divisional manager,as-although incorrect inventory levels were showing on the division's inventory management system and IT services are provided by head office-the source of the error was due to warehouse staff incorrectly adjusting inventories following an inventory count.No adjustment to divisional net profit should be made in respect of this item. The presentation in the performance information of the T$196,000 adverse variance due to the increase in specification of the invertors may suggest that this is uncontrollable by the divisional senior manager because it was required to comply with new environmental regulations.However,though the new regulations came into force in October 20X4,they were announced two years earlier.Assuming the current divisional manager was working for Lochan at that time,he should have been able to lookk for cheaper alternative suppliers before the regulations came into force.Also,T division accepted the price increase without attempting to source from cheaper suppliers.It appears therefore that the divisional manager could have exercised some control over this cost so that this was reflected in the budget.Therefore,no adjustment to divisional net profit should be made for this item. Conclusion The divisional net profit adjusted for items not controllable for the divisional manager is as follows: Adjustment T$'000 Net profit of K division 475 Head office costs 220 Gas price increase 55 Exchange rate (46) Extra costs on 5,000 units High specification invertors 一 Adjusted net profit 704 Adjusted budgeted net profit 690 Variance 14F Though the divisional manager has a financial incentive to argue that the bonus scheme is unfair in order to receive his bonus, which could be significant,it would appear that,in this case,the manage's complaints are justified.If the performance information is adjusted to focus on contrllable profit,then the adjusted actual net profit is greater than the adjusted budgeted net profit as above,meaning the manager should receive his bonus. (b)Transfer pricing policy A transfer pricing policy should encourage divisional autonomy while facilitating divisional performance measurement and encouraging goal congruence between the interests of the individual divisions and the interests of Lochan as a whole.It should aim to achieve the following: Divisional autonomy A transfer pricing policy should promote autonomy of the two divisions.This to ensure that the divisional managers are motivated to improve their own divisional performance,as they will et rewarded for the work which they do and the decisions they make. The price which is set for the invertors to be transferred between the divisions will affect the revenue of Jdivision and the costs of T division and hence the profit of both divisions,which operate as profit centres.Because of this,the divisions will tend to act in a self-interested way,not least for example,because the divisional managers can be awarded a bonus,which is currently based on their divisions achieving the budgeted net profit. A transfer pricing policy must strike a balance between promoting divisional autonomy to set transfer prices and quantities,and the need to prevent divisions acting in a completely self-interested way.Head office should retain some authority to override the divisional managers'decisions. The CFO has said that she will make any final decisions if there is a dispute over bonus payments,as Lochan's head office already did when there was a temporary transfer arrangement several years ago.This was because divisional managers' attention was being distracted from the day-to-day running of the divisions.Divisional autonomy is important to maintain thee motivation of divisional managers.Therefore,by intervening in setting transfer prices,head office is actually reducing divisional managers'autonomy.This could lead to the managers becoming demotivated. Divisional performance measurement The transfer pricing policy must make the divisions see the transfer prices and amounts as fair,commercial prices,otherwise, the divisions may disagree about these,even to the extent that this distracts the divisional managers'attention from other issues in the business,as happened with the temporary transfer pricing arrangement several years ago. The divisional managers are likely to view a transfer price as unfair if it would result in a lower profit than they could achieve by transacting with an external customer or supplier.Also,the manager of T division would think it unfair if the transfer price were to cover some of Jdivision's fixed overheads,for example.This is becauseJ division has spare production capacity and its fixed overheads would,by definition,remain unchanged ifJ division was to transfer to T division.The transfer pricing policy may need to be explicit about which ofJdivision's costs the transfer price should cover,to prevent disagreements between thee divisions about this. The transfer pricing policy relating to T division and J division should be clear in how it deals with exchange rate movements when measuring divisional performance.This is because the two divisions operate in different countries and therefore have different currencies.This will be particularly important because the exchange rate between the two currencies has been volatile and changed significantly over several years.This means that the transfer price could fluctuate widely and will have a significant impact on the revenue which J division receives for its invertors and the cost of these for T division. The cost of the invertors,however,represents approximately only 10%(panels 50%,batteries 40%)of the direct cost of eachh solar panel system,so the impact of these exchange rate movements on the overallresults is likely to be relatively small. Goal congruence The transfer pricing policy should ensure that there is goal congruence to align the interests of the divisions with that of Lochan as a whole.The policy should ensure that decisions which maximise divisional profit also happen to maximise group profit. The policy should do this by encouragingJand T divisions to agree on transfer prices and quantities which maximise Lochan's overall profits and which are in line with Lochan's overall objectives. This did not appear to happen when there was a proposal for a temporary transfer arrangement several years ago.At that time, head office intervened by instructing the managers to abandon this proposal.This was because it was unclear whether thee arrangement was in the interests of Lochan as a whole,because it did not maximise corporate profit.Also,the impact of that proposal could not be reliably measured or administered,because Lochan has a relatively basic IT system. The decision by head office to abandon the previous proposal was also influenced to some extent by a press story that a competitor was fined by the tax authorities in Jayland for setting a low transfer price to reduce its profit earned in Jayland, where the tax rate is 25%,compared to 20%in Teeland. The transfer pricing policy should ensure there is a transfer price which complies with tax regulations in Teeland and Jayland so as to avoid incurring a fine as the competitor did.Within these constraints and provided it is legal and ethical to do so,Lochan could maximise corporate profit by setting a transfer price which would lead to more profits taxed at the lower rate of 20%in Teeland compared to 25%in Jayland. In practice,it will be hard for Lochan's transfer pricing policy to satisfy all three aims,because they may conflict,in particular the need to ensure corporate profit maximisation and sill retain divisional autonomy.Similarly,setting a fair transfer price,by taking exchange rate movements into account,willmake it harder to measure divisional performance.The fact that the invertor comprises approximately only 10%of the direct cost of each solar panel system should be considered when negotiating a transfer price and when designing a transfer pricing policy because the overall impact of this on the results of each division may be relatively small. 3 (a)Simultaneity means that the service is experienced at the same time it is provided.This has implications for the service Ghaill provides as there is no opportunity to rectify an unpleasant ferry experience,be that due to bad weather or the perception which customers have of the service which Ghaill is providing.This is a very different experience to a customer buying a faulty product,which can easily be replaced. Heterogeneity means that each service provided will not be identical for each passenger.This is because the service on thee ferry is being provided by individuals who may not be consistent in the service they provide.Additionally,the service provided may not be perceived by each customer in the same way.For example,someone who sells goods on the ferry may be in different moods on different days depending upon,perhaps,how busy they are,and the manner in which this service is perceived by different customers,or by the same customer on different trips,can be very different Intangibility means that the service Ghaillprovides cannot be touched or held.Instead,travelling with Ghaill willbe an experience which customers will fel and perceive.This may affect the way in which Ghaillchooses to market its services,for example,in that it could choose to focus on the sensation of being close to natural,unspoilt surroundings throughout the trip. Perishability means that the service,unlike a product,cannot be stored for later use.Customers cannot store up the service which Ghaill provides as any given ferry journey starts and finishes in line with its sailing.The service is consumed at the point of its delivery and it is only the sensations and memories of the journey which the consumer can retain. (b) One of the main resources which Ghaill has at its disposal are the ferries themselves.The individual cost of each ferry is not given but it is clear that a ferry is a major capital investment and any business would like to ensure that it is achieving thee maximum output from this asset. In Ghail's case,they undertake 120,000 individual journeys per year with 35 ferries and this equates to 3,429 journeys per ferry per year.Assuming the ferries run for 365 days of the year as indicated,this can be calculated at approximately nine individual journeys per ferry per day. This figure requires a relative measure to make it meaningful for decision making.For example,the journeys per ferry figure for the previous year(s)or,perhaps more meaningfully,the equivalent figure for a close competitor would be helpful. It is also a very general figure and it may be too general to be helpful,given the different lengths of journey which Ghail's ferries undertake and that the relative mix of Ghail's business is unknown.For example,some ferry journeys can take up to eight hours and,clearly,it would be impossible for Ghaillto undertake nine of these journeys per day. It would be necessary to understand the split of Ghail's business and the resource utilisation figure may require to be calculated on an individual journey level,or at least at a level of business operation which relates to the anticipated time of the journey. It is not known exactly how many of Ghaill's journeys are 25 minutes long or around this time.If 90%of Ghail's business is at this level,then nine crossings per day does not seem to represent an efficient use of resources. Another measure which would be helpful to know is the occupancy rate of each ferry.The ferries are of a similar size and have similar capacity but the number of passengers transported in each of the different divisions is not known.If this information was available,this may help to determine the resource utilisation levels in the different divisions. (c) Standards are the benchmarks,or targets,directly linked to performance metrics under headings for each dimension. Equity When setting standards across an organisation,care should be taken to ensure that all managers have equally challenging standards.It appears as though the divisions have different business conditions and that it may be unfair to apply the samee standards across the company.Division B,for example,has the oldest fleet and is much more likely to suffer breakdowns.Thee geographical distance which engineers would have to travel would further penalise the manager of division B with regards to the time taken to repair the ferry.It does not appear atallfair,either,to judge the peformance of divisions A and Don the basis of developing new routes when it would appear there are no routes to discover in their areas. The area of capital investment,which supports the achievement of the standards,does not appear fair either.One of thee divisions could be granted all the available funding in one year which could make it almost impossible for the others to achieve the standards set,for example,in relation to implementing improvements in response to customer suggestions if those improvements require capital investment.It would seem fairer to grant a certain amount of capital expenditure to each division and ask them to compete for larger project funding. Achievability The level at which targets are set should be challenging but achievable,otherwise managers will not be motivated.Some of the standards may not be achievable without guaranteed investment.For example,the improvements suggested by customers may be very different on different journey lengths.These additions could relate to something minor such as ease of boarding on a very short journey to major developments such as entertainment options on longer journeys and could include aspects such as film showings in a cinema or individual film screens in the back of seats. The investment money itself may be hard to obtain as it is clear that the process divisional managers have to go through to obtain funding is onerous and time consuming. Customer satisfaction may also be harder to achieve on longer ferry journeys as inclement weather is perhaps more likely on a longer journey than a shorter one.This may adversely affect customer satisfaction and is an area which the manager of division C(which has a large proportion of long journeys)has no control over. Ownership Managers who participate in the setting of standards are more likely to accept and be motivated by the standards than managers on whom standards are imposed.It is unlikely that the divisional managers feel responsibility for the standards. Perhaps of most significance,they had no involvement atall in setting them and it is natural in such a situation for managers to feel a lack of ownership. It is also clear that the standards are being applied in a consistent and identical manner to divisions which have different business conditions.There is a significant difference between a long and a short ferry journey in terms of the customer expectations of leisure activities and it would seem inappropriate to look to the divisional manager of a division which has a large number of short journeys to be introducing too many customer suggested measures. The investment process is leading to a sense of alienation amongst the managers.They feel that they need the money to invest yet the company is making this money very hard to obtain.This is likely to lead to a lack of ownership as the managers may feel that there is no point in expending the time and effort in putting together an investment bid. Conclusion It is clear that the company should change its target setting and,initill,include the managers in the process.Perhaps an individual investment fund could also be set aside for each division,with larger potential projections having to undergo a more rigorous proposal process.Currently,however,it is clear that the managers do not feel the standards are fair or achievable and, as a result,are unlikely to feel any sense of ownership for them.
